sentences.text: Oh, yes.
You see, because this was just one of the barrack, because in-- in the [GERMAN] where we were, there were 32 barracks.
One on each side.
And when she was thrown out from the back by Lola, then she just came around, and then we met.
I mean, and we hugged and we kissed and we cried.
And as the time was going by, yeah, we were still staying in this barrack where there were no beds, and the water was coming in and everything.
And the people being that is human being, that they were too lazy to go to the lavatory, which you were able to go every morning and every night if you were fast enough, before the [GERMAN] fell.
And some of them, they relieve themself right there.
And the smell was unbearable.
You didn't know which way to step, which way to go.
So I told my sister, said, you know what?
Let's get out of here.
Let's go into another barrack, because if we stay here we will get cholera because-- or the-- or pneumonia or-- or something else.
Had you ever been properly registered with a number?
No.
Not that I recall and not that I remember.
But it must have been that when we were coming out of the shower and this-- we got the dress, they must have asked for our name.
It-- I'm not too sure, but I think they did it.
If not that time, I mean, maybe later on.
So what happened is that we got out of that barrack and we went into another barrack.
And it was no problem because we were three of us, so you always were able to get two other people.
Meantime I had met my cousin, and she had no shoes on.
So I said to her, Alice, what happened to you?
Where is your shoes?
She said, I don't know.
Somebody stole it.
I said, then what happened?
Because the other barracks, they had [POLISH], they call it.
Not bunk bed, but [POLISH].
And this bunk-- Could you describe that to me?
Well, the bunk bed, it mean that when you walked into this barracks, there were a tremendous long, long brick protruding things at the middle of the barrack.
And it had a door at the beginning.
And on both sides of this protruding brick, both side, there was a walkway left to open, and after that there were wooden-- wooden [POLISH].
Wooden-- wooden beds.
And in that bed, they-- they had like four, five pieces of how you call them, board.
And later on, we found out that there were 12 of us that we had to sleep on it from head to foot, head to foot.
So you had no place to put your shoes except under your head.
And if one turn, the other 11 had to because then you had no room.
Like the sardines, we were packed together.
So in the beginning, we were at the middle of one of these [POLISH].
And during the night, you would feel that the urine is coming on top of you because they relieved themself.
They didn't bother to get up from the top to go out.
So then we were-- we were getting the urine right in our face.
So the cursing, what was going on.
Not to mention that every night, the crying.
The screaming.
It was just a madhouse.
It was impossible to sleep.
Then every morning, they would chase you out of the barrack and [SPEAKING GERMAN].

You had maybe two minutes to get into the washroom where they had a tremendous big barrack filled with cement basin and the handles were coming on both sides where you were able to turn it on, and the water was dripping off.
Not rushing off, dripping off.
So at one occasion, we rush-- rushed to the-- the bathroom.
And we were trying to wash ourself, and one of this girl pushed her feet front of my sister, so my sister started to curse and said, you lousy this and this and this.
Where you have the nerve to push your feet right into my face?
So she said, so I have to wash myself too, and you know.
So we turn around and we said, Edith, what are you doing here?
She was our cousin.
My father's sister children, the two girls.
One is Edith and one is [HUNGARIAN].
I don't how you would translate it.
We were so shocked that here we don't recognize each other.
Because I meant to tell you that-- that after they shaved off our head and they gave us this black dress, we looked like people whom we never met before.
We never know.
So I had to watch the dress what my sister wear, and my other sister was wearing, in order to know who they are.
Otherwise you didn't recognize them.
Because we were just distorted.
We weren't the same person anymore without the hair.
What did you do during the day?
During the day, we were talking about cooking and different dishes that how delicious aroma was coming out of the kitchen on a Friday.
And what my mother cooked.
And then another girl would say, oh, I know a very good recipe the way my mother used to make it.
Then another one say, oh, no.
This is the way to do it.
And our mouth is dripping, and our mouth is watering to-- to have all that smell.
Not-- not-- not the food, just the smell.
And the meantime, us we are seated there.
We see that the lice were so big that they were leaving a track as they were passing by.
And one morning, I walked-- I went out before the [GERMAN], and I went near the ambulatory where they were collecting or experimenting with people.
And I had seen this woman front of the-- side of the-- the-- the ambulatory.
And her feet is standing like this.
And gray.
The whole body gray.
And they wrapped in a blanket.
Then I look at the side of the windows, and I see in the windows-- which none of the barrack had windows, but that one had-- there was a glass that had like a little slimy things.
There was another one.
It looked a little bigger, like a little minnow.
And then another glass, there was a little more like a ball sort of slimy thing.
And then the other glass, there was like-- you see that small little thing like a-- like a turtle, like a frog, is all bundled up.
And then the other one, and until finally there was a shape of a baby.
And you were able to see the little nails on the-- on the baby, and it curled up like-- like a ball.
And then you see in the next glass there was a regular baby.
But like-- like a little doll, like a miniature one.
And when I looked at it, I said, where did this come from?
I mean, how-- how it is here?
I couldn't figure it out.
I-- I didn't know that.
Then as the time was passing by, they had ask us around middle of July or something like that, they ask us that we should go to break the rocks which was between the two kitchen.
More to-- as you were coming out of the-- the [GERMAN], it was on the right side, front of the kitchen on the-- on the ground.
They have these tremendous big hammers, and they had smaller hammers and so on.
So I said, who has the strength to pick up such a big hammer?
And there was one woman who was assigned that we should do that.
Oh, I lifted up the big hammer and I was trying to break the rock.
So I see that no how that we could do it.
So I took a smaller hammer, and I was turning the rocks this way, that way, until I find the spot that it broke.
It chipped.
And then I realized that everything has a method.
That if you find a way to do it, you could do it the easy way.
So when I just hit it a little, it crumbled.
It went away.
And as we were seated there, we see the girls are coming for-- with the [HUNGARIAN].
They-- they go going to the kitchen for the nighttime, for the soup.
They were having their arms or they were getting the bread, bringing it back to the [GERMAN], or to the-- to the [GERMAN].
Because every block had two woman, Blockalteste, they call them.
And every three or four bunk bed, they had another-- they called it [GERMAN].
And they were the one who were overseeing that how many people could go up on one [POLISH], how many people could go.
And they were the one who were bringing the bread.
They were the one who were bringing the [HUNGARIAN], the-- the soup.
Sometimes they were asking some of us that we should go and help them and bring it.
So at one-- when-- while we were breaking the rocks, I seen that front of the kitchen on the right side, there is like gray canvas.
And this covered off, and this lined up there.
So I stoop down and I crawled down, and I was going there.
And I lifted up the-- the grayish cover, and I seen some bones in there.
Bones like this, this-- this way.
So I grab three of them, and I was running.

So when I get to the rocks where my sisters, both my sisters were, I started to break the bone up and we took all the-- the marrow, and we were eating it, licking it with our finger, and we were eating it.
Then meantime, we were talking and wondering what kind of a bone is that.
I mean, how-- how did this bone get there?

I'm sorry to say that, but our conclusion was that they were bringing in the limbs of the bodies from the crematorium and they had cooked it into the soup as meat, and these were the bones that they left there.
At one occasion when I was stealing the bone, by that time we got smart.
We were saving the bread from the night before, because we don't get-- we don't want to get sick from this marrow, this fat stuff.
So we were saving the bread.
And to go back at one occasion when I was standing at the end of the-- the lager, I seen a lot of girls are standing there.
And some of the men from the man-- men's lager, they were throwing things.
And they recognize and they know the name, and they know who it is, and so on.
As I'm standing there, I see that something is flying.
And in Auschwitz, you didn't see a bird [? my ?] [? dear ?.] You-- we didn't see a tree, and we didn't see them-- no, I seen a tree.
End of the-- end of the [GERMAN].
But I didn't see no bird.
I never heard a bell being tolled.
And I always said that this must be hell.
That is no civilization, no bird, no bell.
So then as I'm standing, I see something is flying.
So I put my hand up, and I caught a pocket knife.
Do you know what a wealth to have a pocket knife?
And that knife was like one of these Boy Scout knife.
Had all kinds of opening, this screw, that screw, a scissor, and so on.
I mean, I was a rich, wealthy person.
So when I had this knife, that's the knife we had used to slice the bread and we put the marrow on it.
So that's why I'm mentioning it.
So what happened is that after a while, we didn't find the bones there anymore.
And some of the girls just disintegrated from there.
They didn't come there anymore.
So we were only a few handful of people who were breaking the rocks.
So we decided we not going to go there anymore.
So meantime, my--
Yeah.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Only Hungarians.
These were all Hungarians, only the-- the Blockaltestes and the Lageraltestes who were Polish or French or Slovak.
And at one occasion, we were standing [GERMAN] and it was on a Friday.
And as we were standing [GERMAN], there was this woman, Polish woman, with her daughter.
She was the Blockalteste, and her daughter was like the assistant, sort of.
And she had a walking stick, not a-- not a rounded handle.
Just a stick with a very sharp metal end to it.
And as she is counting, you know, in German, she seen this beautiful woman standing on line.
She starts to call her all kinds of whore.
All kinds of dirty language.
You were sleeping under the cover with your lover.
And you were fornicating.
And you were having a good time while I was here.
I was burying my child.
I was cutting as an ice block out of my other daughter, taking her to the crematorium, and you were still having your stomach filled with goose liver, and you were having all the good things.
And as she is talking, she is winding herself up to such an extent.
Then she starts to hit the woman.
And she poked her eyes out.
She poked her eyes out right there.
So the poor woman gave out a scream and she fainted.
And then she just kicked her and throw it.
And she throw her at the end of the-- the barrack where they picked up all of the dead ones and they were taking them to the crematorium.
And later on, I heard that she was lighting candles.
She lighted the candles Friday night.
And then she said, my dear children, behave yourself.
And be good.
Then you won't go through the chimney.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, we never-- we never would-- would think that such a thing exists.
So--
There was one time when the Blockalteste said, this is going to be Yom Kippur.
Do not fast because then you will faint and you will be taken to the crematorium.
And on that particular day, on Yom Kippur, I was walking-- because I was always searching.
I was always walking.
If I stay near the barrack, I see only lice and-- and people who are dying and hunger and so on.
So I figure, let me look around.
Maybe I get something.
So as I was walking towards the end of the barrack-- because end of the [GERMAN] was the trains station-- I mean the train tracks.
Was the trains was coming in.
And this man at the other-- other side of the [GERMAN] there, we called that the Czech.
The Czech lager.
Because when we arrive to Auschwitz, there was a lot of Czech people, families together with their own clothing.
They were in that lager.
So from one night to the next, they emptied the barrack.
But previous to that, I witnessed where one of the Blockalteste was doing business with one of the men from the Czech lager.
The man was throwing big heavy skiing shoes through the fence.
One of them landed all right.
The other one got caught in the fence, so she-- she couldn't get it.
But this Blockalteste was throwing bread over the fence to this guy.
And I had seen a small little blanket, a beautifully designed little blanket, that also this guy had thrown over the fence.
So when I seen that, I said to myself that there is business going on.
But where do you get the extra bread to do business?
I mean, and you have to know the language.
So at that particular day, Yom Kippur, when I was walking at the end of the lager, I seen this man hidden in back of one of the barrack.
And he has a prayer book in his hand, which I've never-- never seen-- I mean, you didn't see paper, pencil.
You didn't see nothing, civilization.
So he start to tell me in Hungarian that I should pray because it's Yom Kippur now, and this and that.
I said, yes.
I know.
My father was praying too, and my mother.
And look.
And all the other religious Jew, and what happened.
The matter of fact, I have to go back.
That my father was so religious-minded that being that we had the big garden and my brother went down at that particular Shabbos and collected all the nuts from under the tree that was fallen.
And when he came up from the garden, my father met him and seen that his bucket was filled with nuts.
He started to hit him.
And he started hollering.
Don't you know what day is today?
Today-- today is Shabbos Shiva.
Shabbos Shivas, you don't go down to the garden.
Shabbos Shivas, you don't collect nothing, and all that.
And I-- I felt that I love my brother very dearly, that what's the big deal?
Shabbos Shivas.
So what did he do?
He just picked up the nuts.
So when this guy at the [GERMAN] when I was, and he was at the Czech lager, and he's telling me that today is Yom Kippur.
It really didn't make any different for me.
Here, they were so religious.
Shabbos Shiva.
So-- so what-- what's that different?
They-- they were all smoked out.
They were all gone on.
So it really didn't-- didn't phase me that is Yom Kippur.
So to go back to my cousin, when I met her and she lost her-- she-- they stole her shoes from her.
I said, you know what?
From now on, you gonna stay with us.
Forget about your friends.
You stay with us.
But previous to that, every time-- I would say every two days, they were selecting us.
First they were selecting the skinny ones.
Then they were selecting the one who was well-built, who was capable and so on.
But there was selection day and night.
You never know what they are selecting for.
There were people who used to say, don't go out at nighttime because if you go out at nighttime, they going to grab you and put you in the crematorium.
Because one of my friend went out, and she never came back, and we never seen her.
I-- I tell you what happened.
We were standing on [GERMAN] and getting-- waiting for our bread and our soup.
And there were these two much older girls than we were standing there from the same town where we come from.
So I said to them, look, my sister is so hungry.
And we are hungry too.
How about if we will, between the seven-- six, seven of us, I said, we will mix up the line that we will have like three lines instead of two.
So we will get an extra bucket of-- of-- pot full of soup, and extra five portion of bread.
She says to me, you're crazy.
You want people not to have bread and not to have soup?
I said, but they are stealing our bread away, these Blockaltestes.
I said, you should see how much bread she was throwing through the fence to this Czech man.
I said, so they are stealing from us.
I said, so if one or two people won't have bread they have inside, they could give it to them.
You crazy.
I could never do that.
I made up my mind from then on if I want to steal or if I want to do something, I do it.
I don't need any judgment.
Because the judgment, I don't go too far.
So it didn't work out.
Then I went, and I had seen after [GERMAN] one day that there were these people we-- you know, as shortage as you have and hardship as you have, there is still a class different.
You are the Hungarian.
They call it the stupid [HUNGARIAN].
Then is the one who are coming from the car path area.
They are the one who are the most resourceful because they were going through hardship before the [GERMAN], and they had to survive.
And they had no income.
They had no food.
The matter of fact, there were times while we were home that we heard that they went to the rabbi and they asked the rabbi, being that we don't have wheat to make matzohs, can we eat corn?
The rabbi said, yes.
That's how bad it was.
So what happened in Auschwitz, there was also a class different.
These people, they'd be called the [HUNGARIAN].
They were like rats.
When the [HUNGARIAN] was coming from the kitchen, they would ran over-- because some of their friends were carrying the [HUNGARIAN]-- and they dig into it, and they ran away.
But the meantime, the one who was carrying it helped them.
Lift up the top, and then that-- and they were-- meantime they are screaming, look, look.
They-- they are taking this.
But I seen it, what was going on.
When I was trying to do it, they beat me up.
I didn't had the chance to go to first base.
But they-- they-- they stuck together.
So one night I had seen that one of them-- it wasn't even dark yet-- that one of them bends down, and the other one stands up and takes the bread out.
Because you know, when you have the barrack, the way the attic is.
And on this side, there is like a ledge.
And some of these newly arrived girls, they said, why should I eat the bread now?
I will save it for morning.
Though ?] they put it up like-- they thought it is on the nice shelf.
And this girl, when I seen that, I said-- I stole the shoes like that because I had seen the shoes was lined up, and I bend down.
My sister Elizabeth was standing on top of me.
And she reached in and she took the shoes out so my cousin had the shoes.
And I was very happy about it.
Because there were plenty dead people whom you could take the shoes away.
I mean, you were able to obtain shoes.
But this was easier for me.
So what happened, when I seen that what this [HUNGARIAN] girls were doing, so when it was getting dark we would circle around and we would look at the area.
Where is the bread far up on the ledge.
And I would bend down.
My sister would get on top of me.
And she would take the bread, and we would run away as fast as we can.
One-- Did you ever find out what was going on in the experimental area?
You said there was-- where you saw the jars?
No.
No.
I had no idea what they are doing there.
The only thing I know that I met a girl whom I knew from next town.
And she came back one day.
I met her front of the kitchen.
And her hair was completely gray.
I looked at her.
I said, what happened to you?
Where were you?
She said one night I went out to go to the bathroom and they called me.
They took me to a place.
They locked me into this room.
And they put a rat into the room with me.
And the meantime, there was some few-- there were some fumes that was coming into the room.
And I don't know how long I was there.
I don't know how-- not-- but I know that when I opened my eyes, the rat was dead.
And I looked at it.
So after that, they opened the door and they took me out.
And they gave me new clothes.
And they feed me.
And they put-- they gave me everything.
And she really looked so well fed, and from whatever experiment they were doing with her, that's what turned her hair completely gray.
Completely.
And she looked like 100 years old.
The girl couldn't be more than 14, 15 years old.
That was one occasion that I accounted.
There were times when they were saying that there were this-- because we were continuously selected.
And there were times when this good looking man who was at the ramp, he selected us also.
And he was always selecting the skinny ones.
And that's when they told me that that's Mengele.
I said, oh, if-- he was there at the ramp.
And they said that he's the most vicious one.
And his father was there also.
That's what they said, that the old Mengele.
Now, how true it was, I don't know.
And--
OK.
